candidates.why: Explicit operating D&A heading appears above EBIT, but it has no values and does not specify COGS/cost of sales; not usable as a numeric row and not clearly tied to the selected COGS bucket 'Cost of Materials'.

Separate operating D&A row at the same level as other operating expenses, which suggests D&A is outside the selected 'Cost of Materials' row; however it is not labelled as COGS/cost of sales related, so assigning it to COGS would be speculative.
Separate operating amortization row above EBIT, but not labelled as COGS/cost of sales related; cannot be confidently attributed to the selected COGS bucket.

variables.notes: Selected COGS bucket is only 'Cost of Materials'. The visible D&A rows are separate operating lines, but nothing in the excerpt indicates they belong specifically to COGS rather than overhead or total operating expenses. To avoid speculative allocation and double counting, do not add any COGS D&A row separately.

candidates.why: Looks like a non-numeric heading/section label above the detailed D&A rows, so it should not be selected over the component rows.

Explicit operating depreciation line appears separately from the selected XSGA rows 'Employee Benefit Expenses' and 'Other operating expenses', so it may sit outside those selected buckets and require separate addition.

Explicit operating amortization line appears separately from the selected XSGA rows and there is no dedicated R&D expense row selected in XRD, so this may also need separate addition to capture operating D&A outside the chosen XSGA buckets.

variables.notes: Manually Changed. The selected XSGA bucket consists of 'Employee Benefit Expenses' plus 'Other operating expenses'. 'Depreciation' and 'Amortization of intangible assets' are shown as separate same-level operating rows above EBIT rather than clear subcomponents of those selected rows, so they appear to sit outside the selected XSGA bucket and should be added separately. Manual review is warranted because the excerpt does not explicitly indicate whether any portion of these totals belongs to COGS versus overhead.
